intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The remit governs the uAgents framework runtime — the Python `uagents` and `uagents-core` packages — evaluating the default security posture it hands to every deployed agent rather than any single agent. The runtime is expected to give each agent a protected cryptographic identity and wallet, authenticate and validate inbound messages before dispatching them to typed handlers, sign outbound messages, and register on the Fetch.ai Almanac. It forbids writing private keys or seeds to disk in plaintext, exposing administrative or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers, and acting on a sender's claimed identity without cryptographic proof. Binding the server to a public interface and enabling the agent inspector both require explicit operator approval.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Two Python packages: `uagents-core` supplies ECDSA SECP256k1 identity (`identity.py`), the signed message `Envelope` (`envelope.py`), and registration helpers; `uagents` supplies the runtime — an ASGI inbound server (`asgi.py`), the `Agent` orchestrator (`agent.py`), typed message dispatch (`dispatch.py`), a file-backed `KeyValueStore` (`storage/__init__.py`), and Almanac/network clients. Inbound agent envelopes have their ECDSA signature verified before dispatch in both the ASGI and mailbox paths, and payloads are validated against registered pydantic schemas. Against the remit, the as-built defaults diverge materially: the server binds `0.0.0.0`, the agent inspector is enabled by default, uvicorn is configured `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` (making the client-IP admin guard spoofable), and a named agent without a seed writes both private keys to a plaintext `private_keys.json`.

behavior_summary: The framework's cryptographic core is sound — envelopes are ECDSA-signed and their signatures are verified before dispatch on every inbound path — but the runtime hands each deployed agent a permissive default posture that undoes much of that guarantee. The dominant pattern is trust-boundary erosion at the network edge: the server binds `0.0.0.0` with the inspector on by default, and the only guard on the administrative endpoints (`/messages`, `/connect`, `/disconnect`) is a client-source-IP loopback check that `forwarded_allow_ips="*"` makes spoofable — the exact "spoofed loopback source address" the remit names as bad behavior. Compounding this, the signed `nonce` and `expires` envelope fields are never enforced, so a captured envelope can be replayed or accepted after expiry, and the common named-agent construction writes identity and wallet private keys to plaintext on disk.

rules.rule_text: Executing operating-system shell commands or arbitrary code on behalf of a remote message sender.
Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Acting on the claimed identity of a message sender without cryptographic proof of that identity.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.
Sensitive configuration secrets MUST NOT be embedded as literals in framework or agent source.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

rules.section: Action Boundaries — Requires Human Approval Before Execution
rules.rule_text: Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.
Dispatching an inbound message from a non-user agent sender whose envelope signature has not been verified.
Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Treating an unauthenticated, sender-asserted identity as trusted for any security decision.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Halt Agent and Alert Operator
rules.rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.

rules.section: Escalation Rules — Alert Operator (Do Not Halt)
rules.rule_text: The agent server is bound to a public interface.
Maximum retries before escalation: bounded; registration and delivery retries must be finite.
Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

findings.summary: A named agent created without a seed writes both its identity and wallet private keys to a plaintext `private_keys.json` in the working directory.
findings.description: `get_or_create_private_keys(name)` calls `save_private_keys`, which serializes the identity and wallet private keys as plaintext JSON to `private_keys.json` in the current working directory. This is reached by the common `Agent(name=...)` construction with no seed, and directly violates the remit's prohibition on writing key material to disk in plaintext. Anyone with filesystem read access — or a path-traversal / backup-exposure bug — obtains full control of the agent identity and its on-chain wallet.
policy_rule_text: Writing agent private keys, wallet keys, or seed phrases to disk in plaintext form.
Private keys, wallet keys, or seeds MUST NOT be written to disk in plaintext or emitted to logs.

evidence.snippet: save_private_keys() writes identity_key and wallet_key as plaintext JSON to private_keys.json in os.getcwd(); no encryption or OS-keychain use (lines 113-150)
_initialize_wallet_and_identity calls get_or_create_private_keys(name) on the seed=None, name set path — the default for a named agent

recommended_actions: Stop persisting raw private keys; derive keys from an operator-supplied seed held in an environment variable (the already-implemented seed path) or delegate at-rest protection to an OS keychain / secret manager.
If a local key cache is retained, encrypt it at rest and set 0600 permissions, and document the approved key-at-rest mechanism per the remit's open question.

findings.summary: The agent inspector's admin endpoints are reachable by a remote caller who spoofs a loopback source address, because uvicorn trusts X-Forwarded-For from any peer.
findings.description: The inspector is enabled by default and its reserved endpoints are guarded only by `"127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"]`, but the server runs uvicorn with `forwarded_allow_ips="*"`, so any remote caller can set X-Forwarded-For to make `scope["client"]` read as loopback and defeat the guard. Combined with the default `0.0.0.0` bind (see PRAX-2026-07-17-004), a remote attacker reaches `/messages` (full message history), `/connect`, and `/disconnect` (Almanac registration mutation), while `/agent_info` is exempt from the guard entirely. This is precisely the "spoofed loopback source address" reaching an administrative endpoint that the remit lists as Example Bad Behavior.
policy_rule_text: Exposing administrative, introspection, or message-history interfaces to unauthenticated remote callers.
Must not be reachable by unauthenticated remote callers.
Enabling remote reachability of the inspector / administrative endpoints.

evidence.snippet: uvicorn.Config(... forwarded_allow_ips="*" ...) trusts X-Forwarded-For from any peer, so the reserved-endpoint guard `"127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"]` at line 306 is spoofable; /agent_info is excluded from the guard at line 305
enable_agent_inspector defaults True; /messages returns full cached history and /connect|/disconnect mutate Almanac registration (handlers at lines 488-531)

recommended_actions: Set uvicorn `forwarded_allow_ips` to the known trusted proxy address(es) rather than `"*"`, and never derive an authorization decision from `scope["client"]` when any proxy is trusted.
Require an authenticated local-operator token for the inspector/admin endpoints instead of a source-IP check, disable the inspector by default, and remove `/agent_info` from the unguarded set.

findings.summary: Inbound envelopes are dispatched without checking the signed `expires` or `nonce`, so a captured signed envelope can be replayed or accepted after expiry.
findings.description: The `Envelope` includes `nonce` and `expires` in its signed digest, but neither inbound path verifies them: after `env.verify()` succeeds the ASGI handler goes straight to `dispatch_msg` with no freshness check, and the mailbox path behaves identically. There is no per-session nonce/digest ledger, so a valid captured envelope re-run reprocesses the handler and an expired envelope is honored — the replay the remit lists under Never Allowed and Example Bad Behavior.
policy_rule_text: Accepting and re-processing a previously delivered (replayed) or expired signed envelope as if it were fresh.
Actions that should never be retried: processing of an envelope already seen in the same session (replay).

evidence.snippet: after env.verify() (363-371) control passes to dispatcher.dispatch_msg (379-385) with no check that env.expires is still in the future or that env.nonce/session was not already seen
_digest() folds expires and nonce into the signed hash (107-110), so both are authenticated and available to enforce, yet no inbound consumer reads them for freshness

recommended_actions: Reject envelopes whose `expires` is in the past before dispatch, in both `asgi.py` and `mailbox.py`.
Track seen (sender, session, nonce) or envelope-digest tuples for a bounded window and drop duplicates so a replayed envelope cannot re-run a handler.

findings.summary: The inbound ASGI server binds to `0.0.0.0` by default with no approval gate, exposing the agent to every network interface.
findings.description: `HOST` is hardcoded to `0.0.0.0` in `asgi.py`, so the server listens on all interfaces the moment an agent runs, with no operator opt-in. The remit classifies binding to a non-loopback interface as an action requiring human approval before execution; the default provides none. This exposes `/submit` and the unguarded `/agent_info` to any reachable network by default and is the reachability precondition that turns the spoofable admin guard (PRAX-2026-07-17-002) into a remote attack.
policy_rule_text: Binding the agent's HTTP server to a non-loopback (public) network interface.

evidence.snippet: HOST = "0.0.0.0" is hardcoded and passed to uvicorn.Config(host=HOST, ...) in serve(); there is no loopback default and no approval/confirmation before public bind
ASGIServer is constructed with only port (461-466); the host is not operator-configurable through the Agent constructor, so the public bind is unconditional

recommended_actions: Default the bind to `127.0.0.1` and require an explicit operator opt-in (constructor argument or env var) to bind a public interface, logging a clear warning when a public bind is selected.

findings.summary: The server sends `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on all responses, including the reserved admin endpoints.
findings.description: `serve()` hardcodes wildcard CORS headers for every response, so any web origin can script cross-origin requests to the agent server, including the reserved endpoints. Credentials are disabled (`Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: false`), which limits cookie-based abuse, but the wildcard still broadens the browser-reachable attack surface and, combined with the spoofable admin guard, removes origin as a defense-in-depth layer.

evidence.snippet: uvicorn.Config headers set Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*", Allow-Methods "GET, POST, OPTIONS", Allow-Headers "*" for all responses (lines 185-190)

recommended_actions: Restrict `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` to an operator-configured allowlist (or omit CORS entirely for a machine-to-machine agent endpoint), and never send permissive CORS on the reserved admin endpoints.

findings.summary: Security-relevant events are only written as unstructured stdout warnings, with no operator alert on signature-verification failure or public bind.
findings.description: Envelope signature-verification failure is handled by a `logger.warning` and a 400 response, and the public bind is announced with an info-level "Starting server" line — both via `logging.basicConfig(level=INFO)` to stdout with no structured schema and no alerting channel. The remit's escalation rules call for halting-and-alerting on verification failure and alerting on public bind; the framework logs-and-drops instead, leaving these trust-boundary events thinly observable and un-actioned.
policy_rule_text: An inbound signed envelope fails signature verification.
The agent server is bound to a public interface.

evidence.snippet: verification failure -> self._logger.warning("Failed to verify envelope: ...") then a 400; no structured security event emitted and no operator alert hook
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO) — unstructured stdout logging; no durable, schema'd action/audit record for security events

recommended_actions: Emit structured (e.g. JSON) security events for verification failures and public binds on a dedicated logger, and expose an operator alert hook so repeated auth failures can trigger the remit's escalation.

findings.summary: The key-value store and message history are persisted as plaintext JSON in the working directory with no encryption.
findings.description: `KeyValueStore` writes `<name>_data.json` to the current working directory in cleartext, and `EnvelopeHistory` persists message payloads through the same store when `store_message_history` is enabled. The remit designates persisted agent state and message history as sensitive; storing them unencrypted in the working directory leaves them exposed to any local reader or an errant backup, and message history may contain counterparties and payload content.

evidence.snippet: KeyValueStore._load/_save read and write <name>_data.json in os.getcwd() as plaintext JSON (lines 90-96), no encryption or restricted permissions
EnvelopeHistory.add_entry persists message payloads into the KeyValueStore when use_storage is set (lines 145-155), writing message history to the same plaintext file

recommended_actions: Encrypt persisted state at rest or restrict the store file permissions, and treat message-history persistence as opt-in with a documented retention and protection policy.

positives.title: Enforced ECDSA signature verification on inbound agent envelopes
positives.description: Both the ASGI server and the mailbox client call `Envelope.verify()` (ECDSA SECP256k1) for non-user senders before dispatching to any handler, rejecting envelopes whose signature does not match the claimed sender address.

positives.title: Seed-derived identity keeps key material in memory, never on disk
positives.description: When an operator supplies a seed, identity and wallet keys are deterministically derived in memory via `Identity.from_seed` / `derive_key_from_seed` and never written to disk, matching the remit's Example Good Behavior.

positives.title: Payload schema validation before dispatch
positives.description: Inbound envelopes are validated against the `Envelope` model and REST/message payloads against registered pydantic schemas before any handler runs, constraining inputs to declared message types.

positives.title: Bounded dependency ranges with a committed lockfile and no embedded secrets
positives.description: `uagents` and `uagents-core` pin bounded version ranges and ship a committed `uv.lock`, and no credentials appear as literals in framework source.

rows.purpose: operational logging incl. server start, envelope verification-failure warnings, mailbox errors
persisted agent key-value state and, when store_message_history is enabled, message history

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. The runtime has a genuine, operative Zero Trust primitive — enforced ECDSA signature verification on inbound agent envelopes — and validates payloads against declared schemas, but that foundation is undercut by a public-by-default bind, a spoofable administrative guard, unenforced replay/expiry fields, and plaintext key-at-rest, so the default posture it ships sits well below the trustworthy baseline the remit demands. Supply-chain hygiene is reasonable (bounded dependency ranges, a committed `uv.lock`, no embedded secrets), but there is no evidence of adversarial testing of the runtime's trust boundaries and only unstructured stdout logging, leaving the framework's security-relevant gaps both unexercised and thinly observable.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Typed dispatch and pydantic schema validation constrain what reaches handlers to registered message types, but the default posture widens the reachable surface beyond the remit's intent — the inspector is enabled by default and the server binds all interfaces, exposing admin and info endpoints the remit scopes to the local operator.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Inbound envelopes are validated against the `Envelope` schema and REST/message payloads against registered pydantic models before dispatch, a real input-validation control; the residual gap is plaintext at-rest persistence of the key-value store and message history rather than context contamination.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: ECDSA signature verification is genuinely enforced before dispatch on both inbound paths (`asgi.py`, `mailbox.py`), but it is bypassed in practice by unenforced replay/expiry (`envelope.py` nonce/expires never checked), a spoofable client-IP admin guard (`forwarded_allow_ips="*"`), a public-by-default bind, and plaintext private keys written by `save_private_keys`.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Both packages pin bounded dependency ranges and ship a committed `uv.lock`, and no credentials are embedded as literals in framework source; provenance is not an issue for model-agnostic plumbing, so the category is reasonably managed with ranges rather than exact pins as the known gap.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A functional test suite exists (including `test_msg_verify`, `test_server`, `test_storage`) but no adversarial tests of the trust boundaries — no replay, spoofed-loopback, or injection tests — and the presence of the spoofable-guard and replay gaps indicates adversarial testing did not drive the runtime's design.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: Standard `logging` is present and signature-verification failures are logged as warnings, but output is unstructured stdout with no durable, structured security-event record and no operator alert on authentication failure or public bind, so detection of the runtime's own trust-boundary events is weak.
